label: ai
topic: A woman's day told in clock-ticks — discovering her husband has died
text:
You wake before the alarm, a thin gray light already pressing at the edges of the blinds. The clock reads 6:12. You lie there for a moment, inventorying the day—lunches to pack, a permission slip to sign, the stain on the living room rug you've been meaning to scrub. Ordinary things, manageable things.

At 7:03, you back out of the driveway with your coffee still too hot to drink. The radio murmurs something forgettable. The street is empty except for a flicker of movement near the curb. You don't register it until it's too late—a blur, a soft impact, a sound that is both nothing and too much. You brake, heart stuttering. In the rearview mirror, there is a small, still shape on the asphalt.

You sit there longer than you should. At 7:05, a car turns onto the street behind you and honks. You move.

By 8:14, the kitchen smells like lemon cleaner and burnt toast. You scrub the counters harder than necessary, as if pressure might erase the image replaying in your head. The house is loud with the small chaos of mornings—backpacks thudding, shoes misplaced, one child insisting they can't find their homework. You nod, answer, move through it all. Your hands keep working.

At 9:27, the house is empty. You stand in the doorway of the living room, looking at the rug. The stain is still there, faint but persistent. You kneel and begin to scrub, the fibers rough beneath your fingers. The clock ticks audibly from the wall, each second marked, undeniable.

At 11:02, your phone buzzes with a message from your husband. Just a question—did you remember to call the insurance company? You type back yes, even though you haven't. You add a heart, then delete it, then add it again. The message sends. You watch the screen for a moment, as if waiting for something else to arrive.

At 1:15, you're in the car again, this time in the school pickup line. The sun is brighter now, the world sharper. Children spill out of the building in waves, loud and alive. Yours climb into the backseat, talking over each other, their words overlapping into something almost musical. You nod at the right moments. You ask the right questions.

At 2:48, you pass the spot on the road without meaning to look, but you do. There's nothing there now. No trace. The asphalt is just asphalt again. You grip the steering wheel a little tighter.

At 4:06, you're folding laundry, pairing socks that never seem to stay matched. The television hums in the background, some show you're not really watching. Your phone rings. An unknown number. You let it go to voicemail.

At 4:09, it rings again. The same number. You hesitate, then answer.

The voice on the other end is careful, measured. They ask your name. They ask if you are somewhere you can sit down. You say yes, though you're still standing, one hand gripping the back of a chair.

At 4:11, you don't fully understand what they're saying. There are words—accident, hospital, I'm so sorry—but they don't assemble into meaning. You ask them to repeat it. They do, slower this time, as if clarity might come with pacing.

At 4:13, you sit. The chair feels unfamiliar beneath you, as if it belongs to someone else's house.

At 4:20, you hang up. You stare at your phone. The screen reflects your face back at you, unchanged.

At 4:32, you call his number. It rings. It rings. It rings. You hang up before it goes to voicemail.

At 4:33, you call again.

At 4:47, you've called three more people. Each conversation is a repetition, a retelling that feels less real each time. Your voice sounds steady, almost detached. You hear yourself saying the words as if you are reciting something memorized.

At 5:12, your children are asking what's for dinner. You open the fridge and stare inside, unable to remember what you were looking for. The light hums softly.

At 6:01, the house is too quiet. Even the clock seems louder now, its ticking sharp, insistent.

At 7:26, you sit on the edge of the bed, your phone still in your hand. Messages have begun to come in—questions, condolences, words that feel both insufficient and overwhelming. You don't respond.

At 8:03, you think of the morning. Of the small, sudden life that ended without warning, without ceremony. The way the world continued, unchanged, indifferent.

At 8:04, the clock ticks.

You listen.
